actions.thought: I am on the button with Ks 6h in a heads-up game. K6o is a solid starting hand in this position. Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot. A 3x open (300 chips) is a standard sizing to establish initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 5h, facing a 3x raise (300 total) from the Button/Small Blind. 85o is a very weak hand with poor playability and is dominated by most of the opponent's raising range. Although I'm getting 3:1 pot odds, playing this trash hand out of position against a larger-than-minimum raise is a losing long-term strategy. The standard play here is to fold.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit on the Button (Small Blind), which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. My goal is to build the pot for value and seize the initiative. Limping allows the Big Blind to realize equity for free with weak hands, which I want to avoid. A standard raise size between 2x and 3x is appropriate here. I will raise to 250 (2.5bb) to extract value from weaker Ax, Kx, Qx, and connecting hands while maintaining a balanced opening range.

I am in the Big Blind with T4s. Pro raised to 250 from the Small Blind. I need to call 150 more into a pot of 350, giving me very good odds to see a flop. T4s has some suited and high card potential in a heads-up match. I will call to defend my blind and see how the flop develops.

I have 10-high on a King-Jack-Trey rainbow board. This board favors the pre-flop aggressor's range significantly. I have no pair and no real draw. Checking is the standard play here to see what the opponent does.

I hold Ace-Queen on a K-J-3 rainbow board. While this board favors my range as the pre-flop raiser, it also connects well with the Big Blind's defending range (Kx, Jx, QT). My hand has decent showdown value (Ace-high) and draws (gutshot to a straight, overcards). Betting here often isolates me against better hands (pairs) while folding out hands I already beat. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see a turn card to potentially hit a Ten, Ace, or Queen, and keeps the pot manageable. It also under-represents my strength, potentially inducing bluffs from the opponent on later streets.

The turn card is a second King, which pairs the board. I currently hold ten-high with no draws and zero showdown value. Since the flop went check-check, it is possible neither of us has much, but as the Big Blind caller, my range is capped. I will check here and likely fold to any significant bet, as I have no equity to continue.

On the Turn, the board is J-K-3-K. The second King pairs the board. My hand (Ah Qs) remains Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). The opponent checked to me again. Since I checked back the flop, my range is somewhat capped, but so is the opponent's. Betting here doesn't extract much value (worse hands fold) and isolates me against trips (Kx) or a pair of Jacks which might call. I have showdown value with Ace-high and legitimate outs to improve on the river (T, A, Q). Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and keeps the pot small, which is preferable given my marginal showdown value.

I have ten-high on a board of Js-Kd-3c-Ks-8s. I have no showdown value whatsoever. Since the flop and turn were checked by both players, it is possible Pro has a very weak hand, but almost any hand in their range (including Ace-high or small pocket pairs) beats my current holding. While a bluff is an option, the board texture isn't particularly favorable for representing a hand that would check-check twice. I will check and hope for a cheap showdown or fold if Pro bets.

On the River, the board is Js Kd 3c Ks 8s. The action has been checked through on the Flop and Turn. The opponent checks to me for a third time. I hold Ace-high (Ah Qs). The 8s completes a possible backdoor flush, and there are three spades on the board. 

My hand has reasonable showdown value in a pot where both players have shown significant weakness. I beat all missed straight draws (like T9, Q9) and weaker Ace-highs. If I bet, I would be turning my hand into a bluff, trying to fold out small pocket pairs or a weak 3/8. However, given the passive line, the opponent might look me up with any pair. Checking back allows me to realize my equity without risking more chips, and Ace-high is frequently good here after a triple check from the Big Blind.

In Hand #3, I am on the Button/Small Blind holding Td 7h. This is a marginal hand but generally worth a raise in heads-up play to take control of the pot and utilize my positional advantage. I will raise to 300, which is 3x the big blind, a standard opening size to test the opponent's reaction and potentially take the blinds immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with A9o, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well ahead of the opponent's likely opening range. The opponent raised to 300 (3BB). Getting 2:1 odds on a call, folding is incorrect due to the strength of my hand. While a 3-bet is an option, calling is standard to keep the opponent's range wide and avoid bloating the pot out of position with a medium-strength Ace. I will call the additional 200 chips to see a flop.

I hold As9h on a wet board of 8c Jc Ts. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen) and an overcard (Ace). As the pre-flop caller out of position, it is standard to check to the aggressor. This board texture (Jack-Ten high with a flush draw) connects well with the Button's opening range (broadways, suited connectors), so donk betting is unlikely to be effective. I will check and evaluate based on the opponent's action.

I have middle pair with a Ten on a very coordinated board (8-J-T with two clubs). This board hits the Big Blind's calling range quite hard with many straight and flush draws, as well as hands like Jx or better Tens. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and see a turn card for free, preserving my stack and utilizing my position to evaluate the turn action.

I hold As9h on a board of 8c Jc Ts 4s. I have an Open-Ended Straight Draw (8-9-T-J, needing a 7 or Q) and an overcard (Ace). My opponent checked back the flop on a very wet texture, which typically indicates a capped range (weak made hands or air) rather than a monster, as sets or two-pair usually bet that flop for protection. While leading (probing) is an option to deny equity, checking is the standard play out of position to either see a free river or call a bet given my significant equity (8 outs for the straight + 3 outs for the Ace). I will check and react to the opponent's action.

I have middle pair (tens) on a highly coordinated board with several straight and flush draws. Pro has checked to me twice, suggesting they may be holding a draw or a weak marginal hand. While my pair has value, it is vulnerable to many river cards. By checking back on the turn, I maintain pot control and ensure I reach the river to see a showdown or evaluate the final board state without being blown off my hand by a check-raise on this wet board.

I hold a Pair of Nines (9h and board 9s) with an Ace kicker on a very coordinated board of 8c Jc Ts 4s 9s. The board contains a 4-card straight (8-9-T-J) and three spades. While I hold the As (blocking the nut flush), I do not have a flush or a straight. The opponent checked back the flop and turn, indicating a capped range, likely weak pairs or air. Betting here has little value as worse hands (air, small pairs) will fold, and better hands (straights, flushes, trips, two pair) will call or raise. Since I have some showdown value, checking is the best play to try to get to showdown cheaply or induce a bluff which I can evaluate based on sizing.

On the river, the 9s completes a Jack-high straight for me (7-8-9-10-J) using the 7 in my hand. While the board is highly coordinated with potential flushes and a higher Queen-high straight possible, Pro has checked on every street, including the river. This passivity suggests they likely do not have a premium hand or a completed flush/straight. I will bet 400 into the 600 pot to extract value from weaker hands like a pair of Jacks or Tens that might feel compelled to call a standard value bet after the action went check-check on previous streets.

I hold a pair of Nines (As9h) on a highly coordinated board of 8c Jc Ts 4s 9s. The river creates a four-card straight (8-9-T-J) and completes a possible spade flush. The opponent checked back both the flop and turn, a line often taken with draw-heavy hands (like Qx or spade draws) or marginal made hands. The river bet of 400 into a pot of 600 on this scary runout strongly polarizes their range to straights, flushes, or bluffs. Given that Qx is a significant part of a button raising range that checks back flops, and my hand beats only pure bluffs while losing to a vast majority of their range (straights, flushes, better pairs), my bluff-catching ability is too weak. Although I hold the As (blocking the nut flush), the probability of the opponent holding a Queen or a made flush is too high to justify a call. Folding is the correct decision.

I am on the Button (SB) with Tc 5h, which is a Ten-Five offsuit. In Heads-Up play, while a wide opening range is standard, T5o falls into the bottom percentage of hands (garbage tier) due to its lack of connectivity and suitedness. It has very poor playability post-flop. Against an opponent who has shown willingness to defend and raise, investing chips with this hand is negative EV. The standard play is to simply fold the small blind.

I am on the button with T2o, which is one of the weakest hands in the range. While playing aggressively from the button is generally optimal in heads-up play, T2o lacks both high-card strength and connectivity. Given that I have already successfully stolen the blinds in Hand 1 and won a pot in Hand 3, I can afford to fold this trash hand and wait for a better spot rather than committing more chips to a low-equity steal or a weak limp.

I am on the Button (SB) with 6c 8h, which is an off-suit one-gapper. In heads-up play, the Button acts first pre-flop and is in position post-flop. While ranges are wider in heads-up, 86o is still in the bottom 30-40% of hands and performs poorly against a calling range. With no specific read that the opponent over-folds his Big Blind, and considering we are 100bb deep where reverse implied odds matter, folding this weak hand is the standard and disciplined play to preserve the stack.

I am on the button with Q2o. In a heads-up or small-field situation, opening wide from the small blind is generally profitable, especially since the opponent has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression in previous hands (Hands 1 and 4). Although Q2o is a weak hand, a standard 3x raise often forces a fold or allows me to take control of the pot early. I will raise to 300.

The opponent has raised to 300 (3BB), which is a larger size than a standard min-raise in Heads-Up play. While J7o is near the median hand strength, it performs poorly out of position, often suffering from reverse implied odds against better Jacks (domination) and having low playability on many board textures. Against a 3x raise, the required equity to call is 33%, but realizing that equity out of position with a weak offsuit Jack is difficult. Since the opponent has demonstrated they do not open 100% of buttons (having folded in Hand #5), I do not need to defend the absolute bottom of my range to prevent them from auto-profiting. Folding here is the disciplined, low-variance play to preserve the stack for better spots.

I am on the Button (SB) holding J2o. In heads-up play, while ranges are wide, J2o is in the bottom 10-15% of hands and is generally considered a fold. It has poor playability and reverse implied odds if I hit a Jack. I have not observed the opponent folding the Big Blind excessively enough to justify a pure bluff raise with this specific holding. Therefore, the standard and disciplined play is to fold.

Js5s is a strong speculative hand in a heads-up scenario. Being on the button, I want to exert pressure and take control of the pot early. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to maintain my aggressive image.

I am holding 8c 5h in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. While heads-up requires defending the BB wide, 85o is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. I need 25% equity to call based on pot odds, but realizing that equity will be difficult against a 3bb open. This hand falls outside the standard defense range against this sizing.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit (AQo) on the button, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play, ranking in the top 5-7% of hands. My goal is to extract value and take the initiative in position. I have previously raised to 250 with strong hands (Hand #2), so maintaining consistent sizing helps disguise my hand strength while charging the opponent to see a flop. Folding is incorrect due to hand strength, and limping is less optimal than raising for value here.

I have a premium starting hand with Ace-Jack suited in a heads-up situation. My opponent opened for 2.5x from the button. I want to build a pot for value and take initiative, so I will 3-bet to 800 chips.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit, a premium hand in heads-up play. The opponent has 3-bet to 800. In heads-up, the Button (me) has position post-flop, which is a significant advantage. A 4-bet here would likely isolate me against a very strong range (QQ+, AK) and fold out hands I dominate. By calling, I keep the opponent's range wide, including bluffs and dominated Ax/broadway hands, and I get to play a pot in position. The price is right; I need to call 550 to win a pot of 1600, requiring about 34% equity, which AQo easily has even against a strong range.

I have the nut flush draw and two overcards on a relatively dry flop. Having 3-bet pre-flop, I maintain the range advantage and initiative. A continuation bet of half the pot is standard here to apply pressure on the opponent's medium-strength hands and to build a pot for when I improve to a flush or top pair.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit and am facing a continuation bet of 800 (half-pot) in a 3-bet pot. Although I have position on the button, the 9d-4h-2d board texture connects better with the opponent's 3-betting range, which likely consists of overpairs (TT+) or strong broadways. My hand is currently just Ace-high, and I lack a diamond for a flush draw, significantly reducing my equity and playability on future streets. Against a tight value range (TT+, AK), I am well behind, and even against AK I am dominated on kickers unless I hit a Queen. Floating here without backdoor equity is loose/negative EV early in the tournament. I choose to fold and preserve my stack.

Holding 5c 4d on the button, this is a bottom-tier hand but I have position and a chip lead. Given Pro's frequent pre-flop folds in recent hands, I will apply pressure with a standard 3x raise to 300 chips to attempt a blind steal. If called, I can use my position to navigate the post-flop play.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a strong hand in heads-up play, significantly ahead of a standard wide button opening range. My opponent has been opening 3x consistently. While a 3-bet is an option, calling is the most prudent play to keep their range wide, avoid bloating the pot while out of position, and realize my equity against their aggressive frequency.

I am out of position on a [3d Js 7d] flop with Ace-Ten offsuit (including the Ace of diamonds). This is a standard check to the pre-flop aggressor. The board texture connects reasonably well with a button opening range (Jacks, flush draws, straight draws), giving the aggressor the range advantage. My hand has showdown value with Ace-high and overcards, plus backdoor nut flush potential, so I intend to check-call a reasonable bet or check down, but leading out (donk betting) would not be optimal here.

I have the initiative as the pre-flop aggressor and am in position. The flop of 3d-Js-7d is relatively dry but contains a Jack and a flush draw. Since Pro checked to me, a standard continuation bet of 50% of the pot (300 chips) is a good way to apply pressure and potentially win the pot immediately, as Pro has been folding frequently to aggression in recent hands.

I hold the Ace of Diamonds and Ten of Clubs on a 3d-Js-7d flop, giving me the nut flush draw and two overcards. My opponent has bet 300 into a pot of 600. This is a standard defense. I have significant equity (approx 9 outs for the flush + 6 outs for overcards, discounting slightly for dirty outs). Calling is preferable to raising here to keep the opponent's bluffing range wide and control the size of the pot while out of position. The price of 300 to win a pot of 900 offers favorable odds compared to my equity.

I have turned the nut flush with the Ace of Diamonds on a [3d Js 7d 8d] board. This is the best possible hand. Being out of position against an aggressive opponent who c-bet the flop, the optimal play is to check. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out his bluffs and announce strength on a scary board. By checking, I allow the opponent to fire a second barrel with lower flushes, sets, straights, or bluffs. If he bets, I plan to check-raise for value. If he checks back, I will bet the river for value.

The turn card 8d is a significant scare card as it completes both potential flush and straight draws. I currently hold 5-high with a 4-high flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6). Since Pro called my continuation bet on the flop, they likely have a piece of the board (like a Jack) or a draw themselves. By checking behind, I take the free card to try and hit my straight or flush without risking a check-raise on a coordinated board, while also keeping the pot size controlled.

I hold the nut flush (Ad) on a board of [3d Js 7d 8d 9c]. My opponent checked back the turn, which signals weakness or pot control, likely holding a one-pair hand (like a Jack) or a missed draw, rather than a strong flush or set. Because the opponent showed weakness on the turn, checking the river risks them checking back for a cheap showdown. To maximize value, I should lead out. A bet of roughly half-pot is sized to target top pair (Jx) or a straight (Tx) that might call defensively but would check back if I checked. I block the straight with my Tc, reducing the likelihood they have it, but making a call from a Jack more likely if I size it correctly.

I currently hold 5-high on a board of 3d-Js-7d-8d-9c. I have no pair, no straight, and no flush. While I was the aggressor on the flop, my check on the turn signaled weakness or a missed draw. Pro's lead-out bet of 550 on the river, following the completion of various straight and flush draws, suggests they have at least a pair or better. Even if Pro is bluffing, my 5-high cannot win at showdown. Given the board texture and my lack of equity, a bluff-raise would be highly risky, and a call is impossible. Folding is the most prudent action.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jh Js) on the Button/Small Blind, which is a premium hand. In previous hands (#2, #10), I have established an opening raise size of 250 chips (2.5 BB) with strong hands like AQ. To maintain range balance and extract value, I will raise to 250 again. This builds the pot immediately and gives my opponent a chance to defend with a wider range or 3-bet, which I can then re-evaluate.

I am in the Big Blind holding Q5o. Pro has raised to 250 from the Button. While the pot odds are roughly 3:1 to call, Q5o is a weak hand to play out of position against a player who has shown a mix of tight and aggressive play. Given the early stage of the match and my slight lead in chips, I will fold this marginal hand and wait for better spots to engage, rather than calling and likely being forced to check-fold on many flops.

T9s is a premium suited connector in heads-up play, particularly from the button. I will raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive button opening range and build a pot with a hand that has great post-flop playability.

I am in the Big Blind with T7o facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. The opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency to open frequently to this size. While T7o has raw equity around 35-40% against a wide range, calling requires me to contribute 200 into a pot that will be 600, offering 33% pot odds. However, realizing this equity out of position with a hand that makes few strong draws and is easily dominated is difficult, especially against a 3x sizing which offers worse odds than a min-raise. T7o is near the bottom of the defending range and often a fold against 3x opens. Preserving chips for better spots or positional advantage is preferred.

In Heads-Up play, King-Four offsuit is a playable hand from the Button (Small Blind). It has decent showdown value and blocks strong King combinations. I want to apply pressure and utilize my position. A min-raise to 200 is an efficient size; it risks the minimum while attempting to steal the blind or build a pot where I have the positional advantage. It also allows me to easily fold if the opponent 3-bets aggressively, given their history (Hand #10).

I am in the Big Blind holding J2o. Pro has min-raised to 200 from the Small Blind. While the pot odds are attractive (100 to call into a 300 pot), J2o is a very weak hand to play out of position. Given that I have a slight chip lead and we are early in the match (Hand 14 of 150), I prefer to fold and wait for better opportunities rather than defending with the bottom of my range and potentially getting into a difficult post-flop situation.

I am holding King-Queen of diamonds on the button, which is an elite starting hand in heads-up play. My standard strategy from this position is to raise to 3x the big blind (300) to build a pot with my range advantage and put pressure on the opponent. This maintains consistency with my previous button entries.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 5s facing a 3x raise (to 300) from the Button. My opponent has been raising frequently (Hands 1, 3, 7, 9, 11, 13) to this size. While the opponent is aggressive, 95o is a very weak hand (bottom ~15% of hands). Against a 3x raise, I am getting 2:1 on a call, requiring roughly 33% equity to break even, but I will be out of position with a hand that has poor playability and reverse implied odds. Even against a wide range, 95o struggles to realize its equity. It is standard to fold this weak holding against a 3bb open.

In a Heads-Up match, 73s is a marginal hand, typically falling within the bottom 25-30% of hands. While suitedness adds some playability, the raw equity is low. I have the option to limp or min-raise to steal the blinds, noting that the opponent 'Flash' has folded to min-raises from the Big Blind previously (Hand 14). However, Flash has also shown aggression with 3-bets (Hand 10). Given my stack size is slightly lower than the opponent's and the hand has poor post-flop playability if called, the low-variance and disciplined play is to fold this specific hand and wait for a better spot to attack the Big Blind.

I am on the button with 9s7d. In heads-up play, maintaining aggression and applying pressure from the button is important. I have consistently raised from the button in previous hands, and 'Pro' has shown a tendency to fold to these opens. While 97 offsuit isn't a premium hand, it is good enough to open for a standard 3x raise to take control of the pot early or win it pre-flop.

Opponent raises to 300 (3bb) from the button. I hold Th5c in the Big Blind. This is a very weak off-suit hand (T5o). Against a 3x raise, I need reasonable equity and playability to defend out of position. I have previously folded better hands like T7o and J7o to this specific opponent's 3x open. The price is too expensive for a hand with such poor post-flop playability and reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am on the Button with T9o, a reasonable hand for heads-up play that connects well with boards. In position, I should be opening a wide range to apply pressure. A min-raise to 200 is a cost-effective way to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with initiative. I have successfully used this sizing before.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. The opponent has made a minimum raise to 200. I need to call 100 more chips to see the flop, which gives me pot odds of 3:1. In heads-up play, J7o is a standard hand to defend against a small button raise. I will call and see a flop.

I have completely missed this flop with Jack-high and no strong draws. I have a very weak backdoor flush draw with the Jack of Diamonds, but that is not enough to lead out. As the Big Blind acting first post-flop, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and evaluate their action. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet.

I hold middle pair (9s) on a Q-9-3 board with a flush draw present. This is a classic 'pot control' situation. My hand has showdown value but is vulnerable to being outkicked or outdrawn. Betting might fold out worse hands that I dominate while isolating me against better hands like Top Pair (Qx). Checking back allows me to realize my equity, keeps the pot small, and induces bluffs from the opponent on later streets. It also balances my checking range. I will check.

On the turn, the 8d is a fantastic card for my hand. I now have a Jack-high flush draw and an open-ended straight draw (needing a Ten or a Six to complete the straight). With approximately 15 outs (9 diamonds for the flush, plus 3 non-diamond Tens and 3 non-diamond Sixes), I have significant equity. Since the opponent checked back the flop, they likely don't have a very strong hand like top pair with a good kicker. However, checking again is a sound strategy to see a free river card or to potentially check-call and realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position.

I hold a pair of Nines (middle pair) on a board of Qd-9c-3d-8d. The turn card (8d) is dangerous as it completes a potential flush draw and connects with straight draws (JT). My hand has showdown value, but it is not strong enough to bet for value given the wet board texture. Betting might isolate me against better hands or force me to fold to a check-raise. Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs or catch a safe river card to get to showdown cheaply.

I have completed a Jack-high flush on the river with the Jd in my hand on a board of Qd 9c 3d 8d Kd. Since there are no pairs on the board, a full house is impossible. The only card that beats my hand is the Ace of diamonds. Given that Pro checked back on both the flop and the turn, it is unlikely they are holding a monster hand like the nut flush, though they could be slow-playing. I have a very strong hand and should bet for value. I will bet 300 into the 400 pot, aiming to get called by a King, a Queen, or a lower diamond.

The board shows four diamonds [Qd 9c 3d 8d Kd], making a flush extremely likely for any hand containing a single diamond. Additionally, the King on the river provides a higher pair than my pair of Nines. The opponent checked the flop and turn but leads out for a 75% pot bet on this dangerous river. My hand (3rd pair, no diamond) loses to any flush, any King, any Queen, and sets. The only hands I beat are pure air bluffs, which are unlikely to fire this size on such a wet board where I could easily have a random high diamond. Pot odds require me to be right 30% of the time, which is doubtful given the board texture and action. Folding is the prudent play.

Holding A2s on the button in a heads-up situation is a very strong hand. It has high card value and excellent post-flop playability due to its nut-flush potential. I have been consistently opening for 300 (3x the big blind) in previous hands to exert pressure and build pots. I will continue this sizing here to maintain consistency in my range and force the opponent to either fold or play out of position against a strong starting hand.

Opponent Flash consistently raises to 300 (3bb) from the button. I am in the Big Blind with K3s. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, offering 2:1 odds (33% equity required). K3s is a suited King which has roughly 54% equity against a random hand and performs well enough against a button opening range to defend. I have folded frequently to these 3x opens recently (e.g., T5, 95, T7, 85), so defending with my stronger suited holdings is necessary to prevent over-folding. Being out of position is a disadvantage, but the hand strength and pot odds justify a call.

I hold Top Pair with a weak kicker (K3s on Kd Jc 7d). While Top Pair is strong in a heads-up pot, my kicker is vulnerable, and the board texture (Broadway cards and flush draw) favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Being out of position, leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands or strong draws, while folding out weaker hands I beat. Checking is the standard optimal play here to induce bluffs or catch calls from weaker pairs if the hand goes to showdown cheaply. I intend to check-call a standard continuation bet.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Deuce of Spades and was called by Pro. The flop of Kd-Jc-7d does not hit my range well as I do not have many diamonds or any pair. Pro checked, which is expected. I have an overcard (the Ace) and some backdoor potential, but this board texture is likely to have connected with a Big Blind's calling range (Kx, Jx, some 7x, and diamond draws). Checking behind here allows me to see a free turn card and potentially realize my equity or bluff on later streets if the board develops favorably, while also controlling the pot size since I currently have no made hand.

I hold Top Pair with a weak kicker (K3s) on a board that now has three diamonds (Kd Jc 7d 2d). My opponent checked back the flop, which typically caps their range (unlikely to have strong Kings or sets), though they could have checked back a flush draw which has now completed. Leading out (donk betting) here is problematic because I likely only get called by better hands (better Kings, flushes) and fold out hands I beat (lower pairs, air). Checking allows me to control the pot size with a marginal made hand, induce bluffs from missed hands, or simply see a cheaper river. Given the dangerous board texture and my weak kicker, pot control is the priority.

I have bottom pair (Twos) with an Ace kicker. The turn brought the 2d, which improves my hand but also puts a possible flush on the board. Pro has checked twice, suggesting they might be weak or trapping. However, bottom pair is not strong enough to bet for value on this board texture (K-J-7-2 with 3 diamonds). Checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my showdown value or potentially improve on the river.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a weak kicker on a board that is now paired with Jacks and has three diamonds (Kd Jc 7d 2d Jh). The opponent has checked back both the flop and the turn. This passive line suggests he likely does not have a strong King, a Set, or a Flush, as those hands usually bet for value or protection on earlier streets. He most likely holds a weak made hand (middle pair, underpair) or air/missed straight draws. Betting myself is 'thin' because worse hands (like 7x or pocket pairs) might fold, and better hands (better Kings, Trips, Flushes) will call or raise. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply to win against his weaker range, or potentially induce a bluff from his air hands. I intend to check and evaluate if he bets, but expect him to check back often.

I have Two Pair (Jacks and Deuces). The board is quite wet with a possible flush and a pair of Jacks. Pro has checked every street, which could indicate weakness or a trap with a Jack. Given the board texture and the way the hand has been played, I have decent showdown value but little to gain by betting, as better hands will call and worse hands will fold.
